posts.caption:
“It is important to me that the woman is not perceived only through her body, but also through her own gaze,” says @adeljanska, whose large scale paintings of doll-like figures are now on display at @rolandoanselmigalerie gallery in a solo show titled Near Field. “I feel that the gaze largely determines who the woman in the painting is.” ⁠
⁠
Familiar yet strange, Janská’s figures appear to dwell in a different dimension, one that is close yet beyond our reach. Humming with tension, each subject seem to carry the weight of something left unsaid. Janská’s women watch and wait for some kind of acknowledgement, their muted chalky pastel hues only accentuating the sense of disquiet.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Janská speaks to @lydunishka about her latest show 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Adéla Janská: Near Field. Artwork by Adéla Janská⁠
1. one as many I, 2026⁠
2. condition orange, 2026⁠
3. loading, 2025⁠
5. private view II, 2026⁠
6. pattern (set) I, 2026⁠
7. near field II, 2026⁠
posts.alt_text:
A young man peers out from a diminutive frame, only 2 x 2, small enough to hold in the palm of one’s hand. In the medium’s expected fashion, he looks directly at us, his expression sober. Under the gaze of his attentive daughter, this young man transforms. He is a superhero or a god, and always a muse.⁠
⁠
These images are contained in Passports, a series of over 300 works by photographer @scarvillek, in which the artist reconfigures her father’s earliest passport photograph. On 11 June, @mack_publishing released a monograph of the series, Scarville’s second with the publisher. ⁠
⁠
“The first iteration of the series was a collage,” says Scarville. She flipped through magazines, looking for “images of other Black and brown people to “infuse the passport photo with [a] multiplicity,” she explains. Working with her father’s image soon became a ritualistic, daily practice. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @aselodie speaks to Scarville about the series 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Keisha Scarville, from Passports (MACK, 2026). Courtesy of MACK and the artist⁠

posts.caption:
Dreamlike, theatrical, irreverent and enticing, @camille_vivier’s work abounds with artistic, cultural and cinematic references. ⁠
⁠
Frequently, these evocations are subtle and layered: a hint of David Lynch, a whisper of Alfred Hitchcock, a sly nod to Cindy Sherman, comic books or Carlo Mollino. Occasionally, they are immediately apparent: her striking, statuesque depictions of women bodybuilders and bold, beautifully staged still lifes, for instance, pay direct homage to Robert Mapplethorpe.⁠
⁠
It was this particular connection that Paris’ Maison Européenne de la Photographie (@mep.paris) was keen to explore when its team first approached Vivier about hosting an exhibition of her work. “It’s a sort of retrospective, but not really because I’m not that old,” the photographer tells @daisyaecw. “More a selection of different works from among my body of work, looking at the key subjects and patterns like the body or anthropomorphic shapes, shape in general … ”⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 by #CamilleVivier © ⁠
1. Camille Vivier, Tjiki II, 2023⁠
2. Camille Vivier, Belt, 2018⁠
3. Camille Vivier, Sophie, Lips, 2018⁠
4. Camille Vivier, Chessy II, 2017⁠
6. Camille Vivier, Deborah Standing in Freud's Cabinet, 2023⁠
7. Camille Vivier, Sculpture in HR Giger's Garden, 2022⁠
8. Camille Vivier, Frog, 2021⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
@keoghdewar’s methods are almost as unorthodox as their clothes. ⁠
⁠
To establish their visual language, the New Zealand-Scottish creative partners gathered 30 of their closest friends for a simple exercise. “We picked out the core garments that we wanted to begin with and asked them to wear them how they normally would,” says @alison_keogh, one half of the label. From there, they observed each person’s singularities and, more importantly, their shared habits.⁠
⁠
While their debut explored a pronounced tension between silhouette and materiality, the sophomore collection leans on colourful technical fabrics. Partly inspired by their relationship with stylist @harry_lambert, and the looks created for #HarryStyles’ ongoing tour, Keogh Dewar shift towards a more functional register. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @pedromvasconcelos speaks with @alison_keogh and @katerosedewar about their new collection, Somewhere Else 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Keogh Dewar Spring/Summer 2027. Photography by @george_eyres_⁠

posts.caption:
“Seven years of grieving” is how @iamkelseylu sums up the period since their debut LP, Blood. ⁠
⁠
Among these moments of personal reckoning, the North Carolina-born, Brooklyn-based cellist, singer, composer and performance artist has kept moving forward creatively, scoring acclaimed A24 drama Earth Mama and the Netflix documentary Daughters, collaborating with Jamie xx and Yves Tumor, and starring alongside Debbie Harry in Gucci’s 2025 Cruise campaign, shot by Nan Goldin. Now Lu is back with their second full-length project, So Help Me God, which arrives accompanied by a film co-directed with BAFTA-winner Savanah Leaf and starring Garance Marillier (Titane, Raw).⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Lu talks @gemsamways through this ambitious second chapter and shares their thoughts on geology, vengeance and mentors.⁠
⁠
📸 Photography by @yumnaaa⁠
⁠
#KelseyLu

posts.caption:
Rest in peace David Hockney. The artist died peacefully at home at the age of 88.⁠
⁠
Hockney was perhaps Britain’s greatest living artist. The Bradford-born Royal Academician graduated from London’s Royal College of Art in 1962, his place in the British pop art scene already secured. To call him prolific would be an understatement. A pluralist long before the term became overused, he moved effortlessly between photo collage, video, multi-canvas landscapes, iPad drawings and opera set design, united by an extraordinary optimism and a singular way of seeing.⁠
⁠
His seductive colours and poised compositions have inspired generations of artists, filmmakers and designers, while holding centuries of art history within their frames. Through paintings, books and an irrepressible curiosity about how we experience the world, Hockney reminded us to look more closely, and with greater joy.⁠
⁠
To honour his work, revisit a digitised version of AnOther Magazine’s Art Project, taken from our Spring/Summer 2021 issue, which takes a new look at David Hockney’s work from 1966 to 1978. Link in bio 📲 ⁠
⁠
📸 #DavidHockney in 1965 © Cecil Beaton Studio Archive, Sotheby's London⁠

posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.

posts.caption:
On 16 June, @pittiuomo_official returns to Fortezza da Basso in Florence. Pitti Uomo is one of the most important international platforms for menswear and has long been a reference point for contemporary menswear through its Guest Designer programme, which invites selected designers to present special collections. This season, @simonerocha_ joins the line-up as Guest Designer for Pitti Uomo 110. London-based Irish designer Simone Rocha will present her first-ever independent menswear show at Pitti Uomo on 18 June.
It is not the first time London-based labels have taken on this role — from @viviennewestwood in 1990 to @jw_anderson, @craig__green , and @walesbonner in more recent years. Today, the editor rounds up London-based designers who have shown collections at Pitti Uomo. Swipe for more details.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 Xavier Bourgeois, @pittiuomo_official
#PittiUomo #LondonDesigners #SimoneRocha

posts.caption:
Hi Piggies! Last November, @SamSmith opened a food account, @samserved. Starting from a kebab shop in Denmark, Smith has wandered across the US, Italy, Japan, and the UK. From curated carousels featuring his own photos and videos to Reels where he vividly explains the dishes, the account showcases a wide range of content formats. All of it carries his signature wit, once again highlighting his deep love for food. The editor plans to visit the three London spots recommended by Smith—@karma_bread, @therivercafelondon and @micksfishbar —soon. Swipe right for more of Sam Smith’s food spots.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @samserved
#SamSmith #Foodie #LondonFoodie

posts.caption:
Marlo Kelly is on the cusp of bona fide international stardom. The angel-faced Australian actor stars in the Emmy Award-nominated sci-fi series #3BodyProblem, and speaks to BAZAAR from Budapest, where she is preparing to start shooting season three. From a school production of ‘Macbeth’ and ‘Home and Away’ to her current role as “the scary lady from the show”, Kelly recounts the humble – and serendipitous – start of her acting career. “It feels like an idea that I was born with,” shares the self-described “theatre nerd”.
Through the link in bio, see @marlokelly photographed back home in Australia and continue reading the digital cover story.
Marlo Kelly wears @ralphlauren and @poloralphlauren on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.
Words: @tessdevivie
Photography: @jamie_.green
Fashion director: @jessicasteuarthoyler
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Videography: @talestudio.co
Hair and makeup: @candiceseptember
Production: @rebeccamooreproductions
Digital director: @ariellekatos
Horses courtesy of @coastalbeachrides
Lamborghini courtesy of @prestige.rentals
posts.alt_text:
“It is the luckiest thing that’s ever happened in my life,” says @marlokelly of the moment a talent agent signed her following her performance as Lady Macbeth in a school production. Upending the usual trope, the actor’s parents were supportive of her prioritising art over academics, on occasion. “One time [in year nine], I was worried about exams as well as a play,” she recalls. “And [my dad] was like, ‘don’t worry about your exams – just learn your lines.”
At the link in bio, continue reading BAZAAR’s interview with the #3BodyProblem star. 

Marlo Kelly wears @ralphlauren and @poloralphlauren on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand. 

Words: @tessdevivie
Photography: @jamie_.green
Fashion director: @jessicasteuarthoyler 
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Videography: @talestudio.co
Hair and makeup: @candiceseptember
Production: @rebeccamooreproductions
Digital director: @ariellekatos

Horses courtesy of @coastalbeachrides 
Lamborghini courtesy of @prestige.rentals

posts.alt_text:
Marlo Kelly is on the cusp of bona fide international stardom. The angel-faced Australian actor stars in the Emmy Award-nominated sci-fi series #3BodyProblem, and speaks to BAZAAR from Budapest, where she is preparing to start shooting season three. From a school production of ‘Macbeth’ and ‘Home and Away’ to her current role as “the scary lady from the show”, Kelly recounts the humble – and serendipitous – start of her acting career. “It feels like an idea that I was born with,” shares the self-described “theatre nerd”. 

Through the link in bio, see @marlokelly photographed back home in Australia and continue reading the digital cover story. 

Marlo Kelly wears @ralphlauren and @poloralphlauren on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption: Join Fashion Director @jessicasteuarthoyler at the unveiling of @loropiana’s FW26/27 presentation. Held at Hong Kong’s Art Pavilion and titled Nomadic Reverie, the presentation was inspired by the themes and sensations of travel, translating the spirit of movement into the Maison’s signature vision of understated luxury.

posts.caption:
For #DavidHockney, style was never just about appearances, it was a way of living in and seeing the world.
Today, we are honouring the wildly influential British artist, who died Friday at 88, who moved across a wide range of disciplines, from painting and drawing to photography, opera and ballet design, film, and works made on iPhones and iPads. A proud Yorkshireman and longtime Angeleno, he captured both the English countryside and California’s sun-drenched vistas while helping redefine figurative art for a new era.
Just last year, the Foundation @louisvuitton in Paris mounted a landmark retrospective of Hockney’s vast output, bringing together more than 400 works spanning seven decades of innovation. He placed queer love, intimacy, and life at the centre of his work, helping broaden who could be seen, and celebrated, in contemporary art.
In a 2011 interview with Harper’s BAZAAR, he reflected on his enduring creative momentum. Today, his influence can be seen across generations of artists, designers and image-makers, a legacy that continues to shape how we see the world.

posts.caption:
On Monday, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced the country was implementing a social media ban for children under 16, set to take effect next spring.
It’s just the latest move in this direction: Malaysia, Indonesia and Australia have implemented similar bans, while states like Florida and Utah have tried to do the same (though Utah’s was halted by a federal judge). Countries including Spain, France and Poland have legislation in the works, while Brazil and Denmark also require some level of consent.
These bans present a new challenge for fashion and beauty marketers, who, in an era of “get ready with me” TikToks, have spent the past several years angling for teen attention.
BoF's Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) takes a closer look at how brands may need to rethink their marketing strategies. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @sincerelyyours
posts.alt_text:
In today's market, cosmetics are largely a mass business, but growth can be found in the luxury sector as shoppers trade up to treat themselves.

The prestige tier is dominated by a mix of makeup artistry labels like MAC Cosmetics and Nars, designer labels like Chanel and Dior and legacy lines like Lancôme and Clinique. Each competes across cosmetic categories, from lipstick to complexion, and all rely on a mix of premium performance and beautiful branding to justify the splurge.

As one of the few fashion brands to command a billion-dollar beauty business, YSL has a shot at eclipsing the competition, if it can fine-tune its ambassador strategy, reinvest in its core categories and embrace digital platforms where makeup is increasingly being sold.

BoF's Brennan Kilbane (@brennankilbane) explores how YSL could win the designer makeup wars. Read the full story #linkinbio

Editor’s note: Euromonitor ranking of prestige colour cosmetics brands according to global retail sales (June 2026)

posts.alt_text:
Shea butter is ubiquitous in beauty, but the nations that produce the raw ingredient have long been shut out of the lion’s share of profits. 

That’s begun to change, as countries like Burkina Faso and Nigeria have cracked down on exports, hoping to hold onto more of shea’s value.

Ezreen Benissan (@itsezreen) interviewed local collectors and experts and found this lofty goal is causing hardship at home. Read more #linkinbio

📷 Getty Images, Shutterstock

posts.caption:
@Hermès unveiled an ultra-luxurious mega-boutique at 166 New Bond Street on Tuesday.
The nearly 2,000-square-meter “maison” — which spans six interconnected townhouses and features a spiral staircase designed by Norman Foster and over 500 artworks — is a big bet on London by “the most British of French houses,” as Hermès executives are fond of calling the company.
Read the full story #linkinbio
✍ @vikramkansara
📷 @valeriesadoun

When it comes to athleisure challengers, California-born brands Alo and Vuori have dominated much of the recent conversation, boasting multi-billion dollar valuations and hundreds of stores between them.
But a new crop of activewear brands that have been gaining buzz among Gen-Z women, some of which are generating triple-digit annual growth at a time when established players like Lululemon and Nike are struggling to retain relevance.
BoF's Cathaleen Chen (@kittykewlcat) explores what's fuelling activewear's next generation of breakout brands. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Anoushka Shankar (@anoushkashankarofficial) has spent three decades building one of the most distinctive bodies of work in contemporary music. She has taken the sitar — an instrument rooted in centuries of North Indian classical tradition — into completely new territory, blending the ancient Hindustani raga system with electronic, flamenco and Western orchestral influences.
At a time when we’re bombarded — wars, looming AI risk, a constant churn of uncertainty — her music is a salve. It carries echoes of long road trips with my family, listening to her father, Ravi Shankar, play the sitar.
But this is not just nostalgia; Shankar’s music is rooted in her personal journey. At BoF VOICES 2025, she explained how she has written from joy, from pain, from outrage — and in each case, the impulse to release something into the world is inseparable from the belief that it will matter to someone. Every act of creation is an act of hope.
“I believe that any creative act is a hopeful act, because we wouldn’t send anything out into the void if we didn’t have a hope and belief that it was gonna reach other people,” says Shankar. “By nature, it is about hope.”
Shankar spoke about how she found her way back to music after prolonged creative numbness following the pandemic, and what the ancient discipline of improvisation has taught her about adapting to a world in constant upheaval.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast

posts.caption:
“My mayor Muslim / My bagel’s Jewish / My Christian Dior / Knicks in four!!!”
A clean sweep of the NBA finals escaped the New York Knicks this week, but the instant virality of those four bars, bellowed into a camera by Queens-native MD Ahnaf Hossain after the team’s Game 1 win, captured the infectious fervour that has made Knicks tickets the hottest commodity in town.
While fewer people seem interested in paying $10,000 for a handbag these days, plenty were willing to pay that much or more to see — and be seen at — a Knicks game.
That kind of mania surely has lessons for a fashion industry aiming to reignite consumer interest after a prolonged downturn.
BoF’s Vikram Alexei Kansara (@vikramkansara) explores how Knicks fever can teach fashion a thing or two about the power of collective experiences, can’t-miss moments and organic celebrity endorsements. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @nyknicks, @nba, @kalshisports, Getty Images

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Make-up Artist: Itika Chugh (@fashionmakeupbyitikachugh)
Hair Artist: Umang Artist (@umang.artist), at Anima Creatives (@animacreatives)
Style Assistant: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Maye Musk is wearing the Ethereal top with an abstract coral reef–inspired belt with sculptural organic textures, by Richa Khemka (@richakhemkalabel)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #MayeMusk
posts.alt_text:
From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Makeup Artist: Savleen Manchanda (@savleenmanchanda)
Hair Artist: Hiral Bhatia (@bbhiral)
Set Design: Nikita Rao (@nikita_315)
Assistant Stylist: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Artist Management: Mili Savla (@milisavla)
Line Producer: Salim Memon (@lineproducer)
Tabu is wearing a shoulder-structured jacket from the Red Thin Line collection by Rajesh Pratap Singh (@rajeshpratapsinghworks), paired with the Colour Me Spring necklace, Eternal Knowing necklace, The Eternal Dance ring, and Flower of Life ring — all by Zoya Jewellery (@zoyajewels).
#HarpersBazaarIndia #TheBeautyIssue #Tabu

posts.caption:
Jennifer Lopez (@jlo)’s wardrobe in ‘Office Romance’ delivers a look that feels aspirational without becoming inaccessible fantasy. Through Gabriela Hearst (@gabrielahearst), Tom Ford (@tomford) and Zimmermann (@zimmermann), the film delivers modern power dressing rooted in confidence and precision. Caroline Duncan (@caroduncan)’s costume design gives Lopez one of her strongest fashion roles in years, with her wearing every look with conviction.
Tap the link in bio to read on.
✍️: Santya ahuja (@santyaahuja)

posts.alt_text:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team. 

Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
A trained ballet and contemporary dancer, our latest cover star Sanya Malhotra (@sanyamalhotra_) is an “unconventional” actor who pirouetted her way into hearts with numerous relatable roles—and many a viral reel—carving a unique niche for herself in the industry along the way. Brides Today gets a glimpse into her universe.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor in chief: Ruchika Mehta (@ruchikamehta05)
Interview: Rewati Rau (@rewatinair) and Shraddha Chowdhury (@shraddha.skc)

Sanya is wearing a dark fuschia bridal lehenga set, Safar Collection Osaa by Adarsh (@osaabyadarsh). All jewellery by, Khurana Jewellery House via Amigos (@khuranajewelleryhouse).
#BridesToday #SanyaMalhotra
posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio. 

Editor in chief: Ruchika Mehta (@ruchikamehta05)
Interview: Shraddha Chowdhury (@shraddha.skc)
Photographer: Taras Taraporvala (@taras84)
Creative Direction: Avantikka Kilachand Raju (@avantikkak)
Styling: Yukti Sodha (@yuktisodha)
Assistant Stylist: Anushree Sardesai (@anushree_sardesai)

Hair Artist: Madhav Trehan (@hairstylist_madhav2.0)

Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Set Design: Janhavi Patwardhan (@artnut_j)

#BridesTodayIndia #BhumiPednekar

posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

a list of some of my all time favourite fashion shows ever 🤍

posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption: It’s a great bag. Love it. People who find it ugly don’t know how to work colors into their wardrobe! This is a neutral in my eyes honey!
posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption: Only 3 designers in the list??... You have to add next to Galliano, McQueen and Lagerfeld designers like Mugler, Cardin, Courreges, Kawakubo, Yamamoto, Miyake, Gaultier, the Best creators
posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption:
the minute i looked at it, the clothes looked digestible. they literally speak the founder’s language. loba by internet it girl cindy kimberly.
but is she the only one wearing them?
is it just another failing influencer brand? did we really need it?
i had a look at the fabric specifications. most of the pieces are 100% polyester or 100% viscose. i have nothing against cindy, but why do brands like this even exist today? to give founders a false sense of security that they’ve “built a brand”?
and it’s so overpriced. why are we charging that much for polyester cut-out dresses?
what exactly is the business strategy behind brands like this? or is there one? have they heard of pricing strategy, market research, or having an actual problem statement? i doubt it.
how do these brands stay in business? or do they?
while i know this sounds like a hate post about cindy or loba, i honestly don’t care enough for it to be one. i was researching influencer brands, and this felt like the perfect case study: someone whose entire personal brand is built around conventional beauty, aesthetics, and style, yet still hasn’t managed to translate that into a genuinely successful business venture which tries to copy exactly that.
successful influencer brands are as rare as they come. this is like proof of that.
posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption: Have you missed her? Zendaya made a chic return to the red carpet on Monday, stepping out for the first official photo call for her upcoming sure-to-be blockbuster “Spider Man: Brand New Day” alongside her partner and costar, Tom Holland. The pair coordinated in matching black, spider-centric looks with Zendaya wearing an asymmetrical dress from @christiancowan and Holland in custom @jacquemus.
posts.alt_text:
For a mile-long stretch of Bushwick, Brooklyn, a sea of red, white and blue floods the streets. 

One corner boasts a DIY speaker setup blasting old-school reggaeton courtesy of Tego Calderón and Plan B, another plays throwback salsa from Héctor Lavoe and El Gran Combo. 

Booming horns pulse through the air; vendors pour “piragua” snow cones and serve watermelon and coconut ice cream to those looking to cool down; and elders kick back in personal lawn chairs taking in the festivities, which include surprise appearances from @nycmayor Zohran Mamdani and @nyknicks star (and newly crowned NBA champion) Jose Alvarado (@therealgta.) 

Despite the heat and humidity, a palpable sense of pride ripples through the crowd. This is, after all, what fuels New York City’s Puerto Rican Day Parade weekend, year after year.

The Bushwick event coincides with a long-running parade that has seen Puerto Rican New Yorkers (or “Nuyoricans”) take to Manhattan’s streets every second Sunday in June since 1958. 

“There’re more Puerto Ricans in the US than there are on the island right now. Now that I’ve been here in the city for 13 years, there’s a lot of longing to be back home,” says Monica Santos Gil, the founder of New York-based handbag brand Santos by Monica and a passionate festival-goer. “For those who maybe feel less connected to the culture of the island, the parade is such an incredible opportunity for Puerto Ricans to come together, celebrate that connection and feel close to home.”

📹 : CNN
📸: David Dee Delgado for CNN

posts.caption:
Susan Mah knew exactly what she wanted for her version of a "little black dress." It was the late 1940s in California, and after years of commissioning some of the finest tailors back home in Shanghai and Hong Kong, she had learned a thing or two about making clothes herself.
That's how one of the most surprising pieces of her wardrobe came to be: a cheongsam, or qipao, with a typical Mandarin collar, short sleeves and knee-length, form-fitting silhouette, but cut from — instead of a sumptuous textile featuring Chinese motifs — a bold print of lime green, Mayan-inspired symbols. The Mayan revival cheongsam is one of over 70 stunning examples of early-to-mid-20th century Chinese clothing displayed in "Fashioning Chinese Women: Empire to Modernity," an exhibition opening Sunday at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA).
"I think, had she stayed in China… she would have had to dress very conservatively," speculates her daughter-in-law Chere Lai Mah, 78, who in the decades since Susan's passing has studied the hundreds of personal garments she left behind, building a picture from oral stories and details she has collected from family members, and even the wearer. "But in Fresno, California she wanted to be interestingly dressed, inspired by Irene Dunne and Barbara Stanwyck, so she started to design these hybrid Chinese American cheongsam," said Lai Mah, adding that she would go shopping for the "craziest American novelty fabrics."
The majority of items on show are from a collection that Lai Mah donated to the museum in 2022 comprising mostly of dresses belonging to Susan, as well as some pieces from her own mother, Li Zhang Huifang, who was a good friend of Susan's.
"The collection documents this period of incredible change that women are experiencing," said the show's guest curator, Michaela Hansen, referring to social liberation and mobility many women experienced following the fall of the Qing dynasty in 1912.
Tap the link in our bio to read more.
📸: Grace Widyatmadja/CNN/Chere Lai Mah

posts.caption:
British painter David Hockney, whose vibrant portraits and sun-drenched depictions of the everyday made him one of contemporary art's most beloved figures, has died at 88.
The artist died "peacefully at home" on Thursday, one month short of his 89th birthday, according to a statement provided to CNN by his longtime publicist.
Some of his best-known works are a series of light-filled swimming pool paintings that seemed to freeze a moment in time. But his oeuvre was diverse, spanning photography, printmaking and stage design for ballet and opera productions.
In 2018, "Portrait of an Artist (Pool with Two Figures)" fetched $90.3 million to become (if only briefly) the most expensive work by a living artist ever to sell at auction.
Tap the link in bio for more.
📸 : Thomas Coex/AFP/Getty Images

posts.alt_text:
There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

Photographer: @laaapavi
Makeup: @pircillapae (@aframe_agency)
Hair: @eduardomendezhair (@aframe_agency)

posts.caption:
Before the term “It girl” was recycled into a new trend every few months, Lily-Rose Depp had already become the internet’s latest obsession.
At just 15, she seemed to appear everywhere at once. Not through carefully orchestrated branding or viral content, but through a kind of effortless presence that felt increasingly rare. As the daughter of Johnny Depp and Vanessa Paradis, she arrived with undeniable visibility, yet what fascinated people wasn’t her last name, it was the way she carried it.
Her early Instagram felt refreshingly ordinary. Grainy selfies, photos with friends, school moments, blurry snapshots, and the occasional glimpse into a world most people could only imagine. There was no obvious strategy behind it. No polished influencer aesthetic. No attempt to constantly explain herself.
At the same time, she was quietly stepping into fashion. By her mid-teens, she had become one of Chanel’s youngest ambassadors, frequently photographed alongside Karl Lagerfeld. Yet despite the industry’s attention, she never felt inaccessible. She existed in a space between celebrity and normality, glamour and teenage awkwardness.
That balance made her endlessly compelling.
Long before TikTok trends and carefully curated personal brands became the norm, Lily-Rose represented a new kind of cool: understated, slightly mysterious, and completely unforced. People weren’t just interested in what she was doing, they were interested in who she was.
Looking back, it’s easy to see why the internet became so fascinated. Lily-Rose Depp didn’t break the internet because she was trying to. She did it because, for a brief moment, she embodied something social media rarely allows anymore: authenticity.
posts.alt_text:
Vintage Roberto Cavalli is having a moment again and it couldn’t feel more timely.

For the past few years, fashion has been dominated by restraint. Quiet luxury, clean lines, neutral palettes, and an obsession with looking effortlessly expensive. But lately, the mood has started to shift.

People want personality again.

They want color, drama, prints, embellishment, and clothes that don’t apologize for taking up space. And that’s exactly where Roberto Cavalli’s early 2000s archive comes in.

Long before maximalism became a trend cycle, Cavalli built an entire universe around excess. Animal prints, saturated colors, body-skimming silhouettes, low-rise cuts, and an unapologetically glamorous attitude defined collections that felt designed to be noticed.

Particularly in the early 2000s, Cavalli mastered a kind of jet-set glamour that feels surprisingly relevant today. His dresses weren’t subtle, his prints weren’t restrained, and that was precisely the point.

As fashion slowly moves away from minimalism, a new generation is rediscovering the appeal of clothes with character. Not everything needs to be quiet. Not everything needs to blend in.

Maybe that’s why vintage Cavalli feels so exciting again.

Maybe we don’t want quiet luxury this summer.

Maybe we want Roberto Cavalli.

posts.caption:
Photographed by Tim Walker and guest-edited by John Galliano, the story brings together Galliano and Kate Moss in a theatrical world inspired by the Ballets Russes performance, and fantasy. Throughout the editorial, Moss transforms into a series of characters, moving between reality and imagination with the ease that has made her one of fashion’s most enduring muses.
Walker’s signature visual language, romantic, surreal, and richly cinematic, elevates the story beyond fashion imagery. Rather than focusing solely on the clothes, Fantasia celebrates creativity itself, exploring the relationship between designer and muse, art and performance, fantasy and reality.
The result is an editorial that feels timeless: a meeting of some of fashion’s most influential creative figures, united by a shared love of storytelling and transformation. More than a decade later, Fantasia remains a reminder of fashion’s ability to create entire worlds through image alone.
Editorial: Fantasia
Magazine: British Vogue, December 2013
Photographer: Tim Walker
Guest Fashion Editor: John Galliano
Fashion Editor/Stylist: Kate Phelan
Model: Kate Moss
Hair: Sam McKnight
Makeup: Sam Bryant
Manicure: Anatole Rainey

posts.caption:
A little collection of our favorite women doing summer right.
Taken from old paparazzi shots and beachside snapshots, these images capture a version of celebrity culture that feels refreshingly effortless. No red carpets, no campaigns, no carefully constructed personas—just long days in the sun, oversized sunglasses, salty hair, and the kind of confidence that can’t be styled.
What makes these photos so compelling is their authenticity. They feel spontaneous, imperfect, and completely unbothered. A reminder that sometimes the best fashion moments happen far away from the runway.
posts.alt_text:
Before the term “It girl” was recycled into a new trend every few months, Lily-Rose Depp had already become the internet’s latest obsession.

At just 15, she seemed to appear everywhere at once. Not through carefully orchestrated branding or viral content, but through a kind of effortless presence that felt increasingly rare. As the daughter of Johnny Depp and Vanessa Paradis, she arrived with undeniable visibility, yet what fascinated people wasn’t her last name, it was the way she carried it.

Her early Instagram felt refreshingly ordinary. Grainy selfies, photos with friends, school moments, blurry snapshots, and the occasional glimpse into a world most people could only imagine. There was no obvious strategy behind it. No polished influencer aesthetic. No attempt to constantly explain herself.

At the same time, she was quietly stepping into fashion. By her mid-teens, she had become one of Chanel’s youngest ambassadors, frequently photographed alongside Karl Lagerfeld. Yet despite the industry’s attention, she never felt inaccessible. She existed in a space between celebrity and normality, glamour and teenage awkwardness.

That balance made her endlessly compelling.

Long before TikTok trends and carefully curated personal brands became the norm, Lily-Rose represented a new kind of cool: understated, slightly mysterious, and completely unforced. People weren’t just interested in what she was doing, they were interested in who she was.

Looking back, it’s easy to see why the internet became so fascinated. Lily-Rose Depp didn’t break the internet because she was trying to. She did it because, for a brief moment, she embodied something social media rarely allows anymore: authenticity.

posts.caption:
A glimpse behind the scenes of Chanel’s Fall 2010 campaign, captured on Orchard Street in New York’s Lower East Side.
Starring Freja Beha Erichsen, Abbey Lee Kershaw, and Brad Kroenig, the campaign reflected Karl Lagerfeld’s vision of Chanel through a distinctly urban lens. Photographed by Lagerfeld himself and styled by Alex White, the images blended effortless cool with the house’s unmistakable elegance.
Hair was created by Sam McKnight, makeup by Peter Philips, with behind-the-scenes photography by Rachel Scroggins and Wendy Lam.

posts.caption:
A selection of looks that stayed on our minds this week.
Pulled from old paparazzi images, backstage moments, runway archives, and forgotten corners of the internet, these are the outfits we found ourselves returning to again and again.
Some lean into chaos, others into simplicity. Tiny knits, metallic textures, oversized sunglasses, low-slung tailoring, worn-in boots, layered jewelry, and makeup that looks better slightly undone. Different eras, different aesthetics, but all connected by a distinct sense of individuality.
What draws us in is their authenticity. Nothing feels forced, overly polished, or calculated. Many of these looks ignore conventional styling rules entirely—and that’s exactly what makes them memorable.
The outfits we couldn’t stop thinking about this week.
If you recognize yourself in any of these images, please send us a message so we can properly credit and tag you.

posts.caption:
It was back in 2023 that Arrosa originally founded her club night @necropolisfestival (meaning “city of the dead” in Greek), an underground death metal and techno rave that catered to London’s most hardcore in a disused church from 1563. It was from that night that the Vampire Ball was born – Necropolis’ themed sister event that focused solely on the mythic creatures. ⁠
⁠
Held a stone’s throw from the Thames at The Steel Yard, May’s edition saw hundreds descend in spooky garb that drew from multiple generations of vampire lore. There were the old-timey ones in corsets and Victorian mourning veils, modern iterations in leather harnesses and Rick Owens boots, plus undead kawaii girls in anime shirts with blood-red contact lenses. ⁠
⁠
Swipe for all the best looks from the night ➡️⁠
⁠
✍️ @elliothoste⁠
📸 @heloisedarcie⁠
⁠
#DazedFashion #DazedBeauty⁠

posts.caption:
“Do you feel free London?” @fkatwigs screams to 20,000 of her fans at her Body High tour at the O2 Arena, who scream back just as enthusiastically. ⁠
⁠
Eusexua is freedom. In a world where we lack so much of it, chained to our desks and worldly responsibilities; where we see pleasure as frivolous, even though we all need and deserve it – eusexua demands that we feel more, that we feel everything. On her Body High Tour, twigs asks her fans to have an evening of unadulterated pleasure and freedom with her; queer freedom, sexual freedom and the freedom to express who you are. ⁠
⁠
Dazed clubber and photographer Klara Ähman photographed the singer’s fourth concert tour ➡️⁠

The future of natural diamonds won’t be won through supply alone.
It will be won through meaning.
ALROSA’s newly approved 2030 promotion strategy signals a major shift in focus — from production and pricing to storytelling, education, transparency and consumer confidence.
The question is no longer:
“Can the industry sell more diamonds?”
It’s:
“Can the industry communicate why they matter?”
💬 What will drive natural diamond demand the most over the next decade?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #ALROSA #DiamondIndustry #DiamondRetail
posts.alt_text: An evening of inspiration, gratitude, and humanity—celebrating remarkable individuals whose selfless contributions continue to uplift lives and shape a better tomorrow. Here’s a glimpse of the Santokbaa Humanitarian Award @santokbaa.award by @srk.one

posts.caption:
Diamonds are among the few objects on Earth that will outlast all of us. That raises a profound question:
Do we ever truly own a diamond?
In her compelling essay, Canada-based visual artist Reena Ahluwalia introduces the Billion-Year Paradox  a philosophical framework that redefines diamonds not as possessions, but as enduring witnesses to human stories.
Key Takeaways for the Jewellery Industry
💎 A natural diamond is a 3-billion-year geological miracle.
💎 Human ownership is temporary. Stewardship is enduring.
💎 In a world of deepfakes and digital noise, diamonds represent authenticity.
💎 Storytelling, provenance, and emotional relevance are becoming the true drivers of value.
💎 The brands that win will be those that connect fleeting moments to timeless meaning.
We never own diamonds. We only borrow their forever.
How does your brand communicate the enduring meaning of natural diamonds?
#NaturalDiamonds #DiamondWorld #JewelleryRetail #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
Let's think for a moment... Lab-grown diamonds were invented in the 1950s through HPHT by H. Tracy Hall. Later, diamond CVD emerged through the pioneering work of brilliant scientists such as Boris Derjaguin, William G. Eversole, Nobuo Setaka, Satoshi Matsumoto, Angus C. Wilson, John E. Butler, and many others.
More recently, generations of exceptional researchers have refined these technologies to the point where we can now even choose the colors of single-crystal diamonds, no longer depending on geological events, environmental destruction, or human exploitation to obtain gorgeous gemstones and develop new technologies based on diamond's extraordinary material properties.
To come across an Instagram post claiming that this is not history made is shameful, to say the least.

posts.caption:
Imagine having a glimpse into Charli XCX’s mind as she was getting ready to launch her ‘Rock Music’ video, or into Paige Buecker’s process as she prepared to step onto the WNBA court. @Coach’s new storytelling platform, &Coach, is collaborating with Gen Z activists, artists, actors, and athletes to do just that.
Launching today, &Coach’s line up of co-authors is stacked with impressive names that resonate with Gen Z in particular, like Charli XCX, PinkPantheress, Malala Yousafzai, Paige Bueckers, Angel Reese, Toni Breidinger, Avantika, and KiiiKiii. Via &Coach, these stars will have the opportunity to share intimate moments in their lives and careers, particularly those centered on transitions and monumental events—or, “the uncertainty before stepping into something new,” as Coach describes them.
Iga Świątek celebrating with dessert after the French Open and PinkPantheress taking stage in Brooklyn are a few of the moments fans will enjoy in this first installment, and to make the most of these scenes, they will be viewed through the lens of notable young directors and photographers like Terrence O’Connor, Sage Adams, and Anthony Po.
Rather than focusing only on building an outward identity with a campaign, &Coach seems to be an invitation to the brand’s base to look inward. In other words, while other brands might offer images focused solely on grandeur and opulence, Coach aims to encourage self-expression and personal creativity through this new model which they call “more than a campaign.” These are virtues that Coach’s Chief Marketing Officer, Joon Silverstein, believes are key to connecting with Gen Z.
“This generation doesn’t want to inherit a prescribed version of aspiration—they want to shape identity for themselves, and they want to be part of the process,” Silverstein says. “&Coach was built by bringing creators, cultural voices, and hundreds of consumers into the work from day one.”
&Coach comes to life today on social media at @and.coach
#CoachPartner #coach #fashion #andcoach
posts.alt_text:
Tyra Banks isn’t a fan of her villain edit.

The former executive producer and host of ‘America’s Next Top Model’ is suing Netflix over her portrayal in the viral docuseries ‘Reality Check: Inside America’s Next Top Model.’

In a lawsuit filed Saturday, Banks alleges producers used “selective editing, deliberate omission, and surgical manipulation” to create what she calls a “false and defamatory narrative.” She says that of the three-and-a-half-hour interview she gave for the series, only 16 minutes made it in and all the accountability she took “ended up on the cutting room floor.”

The suit challenges several moments featured in the documentary, including Shandi Sullivan’s account of her experience on Cycle 2. Banks argues that Sullivan’s version of events has changed over time and claims that she “would have addressed the situation in a vastly different way,” adding she “had no awareness whatsoever that Ms. Sullivan’s experience in Milan might have involved anything other than a regrettable, but consensual encounter.”

Banks is also upset that producers allegedly failed to tell her that Jay Manuel, Miss J Alexander, and Nigel Barker were not only interview subjects but also credited consultants on the project. According to the suit, that information “would have raised a red flag” and “she would not have participated.”

Another claim Banks calls “the most hurtful example” involves the documentary’s suggestion that she didn’t support Miss J after his stroke. Banks says she has been living in Australia, tried to locate him and has receipts to prove it. She also says she and Miss J have had some contact. “They spoke live on the phone at least once…They texted numerous times.”

The lawsuit essentially argues that Netflix crafted a convenient narrative. Hmm, sounds familiar. Guess there really are “no sacred cows.”  

Swipe through for some of the lawsuit’s messiest allegations.

#antm #tyrabanks #tv #fashion #news

posts.alt_text:
Imagine having a glimpse into Charli XCX’s mind as she was getting ready to launch her ‘Rock Music’ video, or into Paige Buecker’s process as she prepared to step onto the WNBA court. @Coach’s new storytelling platform, &Coach, is collaborating with Gen Z activists, artists, actors, and athletes to do just that.

Launching today, &Coach’s line up of co-authors is stacked with impressive names that resonate with Gen Z in particular, like Charli XCX, PinkPantheress, Malala Yousafzai, Paige Bueckers, Angel Reese, Toni Breidinger, Avantika, and KiiiKiii. Via &Coach, these stars will have the opportunity to share intimate moments in their lives and careers, particularly those centered on transitions and monumental events—or, “the uncertainty before stepping into something new,” as Coach describes them. 

Iga Świątek celebrating with dessert after the French Open and PinkPantheress taking stage in Brooklyn are a few of the moments fans will enjoy in this first installment, and to make the most of these scenes, they will be viewed through the lens of notable young directors and photographers like Terrence O’Connor, Sage Adams, and Anthony Po.  

Rather than focusing only on building an outward identity with a campaign, &Coach seems to be an invitation to the brand’s base to look inward. In other words, while other brands might offer images focused solely on grandeur and opulence, Coach aims to encourage self-expression and personal creativity through this new model which they call “more than a campaign.” These are virtues that Coach’s Chief Marketing Officer, Joon Silverstein, believes are key to connecting with Gen Z.

“This generation doesn’t want to inherit a prescribed version of aspiration—they want to shape identity for themselves, and they want to be part of the process,” Silverstein says. “&Coach was built by bringing creators, cultural voices, and hundreds of consumers into the work from day one.”

&Coach comes to life today on social media at @and.coach 

#CoachPartner #coach #fashion #andcoach

posts.caption:
Betsy Ross is turning in her grave.
American flag-inspired looks for the “Octagon Girls,” the official ring card models for the UFC, have been revealed for Sunday’s fight on the White House lawn.
“The inspiration was born from the intersection of sport, spectacle, and celebration,” costume designer Marina Toybina told Maxim. Toybina is a seven-time Emmy winner, winning three alone for her work on ‘The Masked Singer’...lol.
“Working closely with UFC, we wanted to create looks that honored both the energy of the brand and the significance of the 250th anniversary celebration.”
The sparkly looks include hot pants, bustiers, and multiple flag code violations. According to US Code Title 4 Chapter 1, “the flag should never be used as wearing apparel” and “no part of the flag should ever be used as a costume or athletic uniform.”
The images are unsurprisingly being dragged online. “Looks like a failed design challenge on Project Runway,” wrote one Threads user. “Tacky outfits for a tacky event being hosted by a tacky administration,” wrote another.
This weekend’s Freedom 250 fight will take place on Trump’s 80th birthday, which also happens to be Flag Day.
#flag #costume #news #threads #viral

Launching today, &Coach’s line up of co-authors is stacked with impressive names that resonate with Gen Z in particular, like Charli XCX, PinkPantheress, Malala Yousafzai, Paige Bueckers, Angel Reese, Toni Breidinger, Avantika, and KiiiKiii. Via &Coach, these stars will have the opportunity to share intimate moments in their lives and careers, particularly those centered on transitions and monumental events—or, “the uncertainty before stepping into something new,” as Coach describes them. 

Iga Świątek celebrating with dessert after the French Open and PinkPantheress taking stage in Brooklyn are a few of the moments fans will enjoy in this first installment, and to make the most of these scenes, they will be viewed through the lens of notable young directors and photographers like Terrence O’Connor, Sage Adams, and Anthony Po.  

Rather than focusing only on building an outward identity with a campaign, &Coach seems to be an invitation to the brand’s base to look inward. In other words, while other brands might offer images focused solely on grandeur and opulence, Coach aims to encourage self-expression and personal creativity through this new model which they call “more than a campaign.” These are virtues that Coach’s Chief Marketing Officer, Joon Silverstein, believes are key to connecting with Gen Z.

“This generation doesn’t want to inherit a prescribed version of aspiration—they want to shape identity for themselves, and they want to be part of the process,” Silverstein says. “&Coach was built by bringing creators, cultural voices, and hundreds of consumers into the work from day one.”

&Coach comes to life today on social media at @and.coach 

#CoachPartner #coach #fashion #andcoach

posts.caption:
To promote her new album which comes out in July, Madonna released a short film called ‘Confessions II’ yesterday on YouTube. The film, directed by the creative production company ‘Torso’ which is also responsible for Charli XCX’s new SS26 video, premiered last week to an exclusive audience at Tribeca film festival.
Madonna recommends watching multiple times due to the dizzying amount of easter eggs and references included within. Fans are taking the hint; Already, Madonna lovers have dissected the ten-ish minute film, closely examining everything from celebrity cameos to a host of discreet audio samples from other hit songs.
One user notes ‘Danceterias’ use of a melody from Lou Reed’s ‘Walk on the Wild Side’, for example, while others note the use of the iconic beat from ‘Lucky Star.’ A Julia Garner cameo nods to the potential upcoming Madonna biopic which was shelved in 2023 and is now in Hollywood limbo. A scene with Sabrina Carpenter suggests Madonna’s proclivity for passing the popstar torch, as she did with Britney Spears at the VMA’s in 2003.
But the most jaw-dropping imagery in the film comes in the form of green laser beams shooting out of Madonna’s dancers’ orifices. Vaginal laser beams… What’s the reference there? Maybe there isn’t an easter egg in that scene, except to say that Madonna has always been a provocateur. She built a career on it, dancing in a wedding dress to ‘Like a Virgin’ and wearing Jean Paul Gaultier’s cone bra on tour in the 90s. Cone bras don’t shock audiences like they did thirty years ago, so perhaps the modern equivalent (if there is one) is vaginal/anal laser beams.
We won’t soon forget the image of these lasers, partly because of where they are emanating from, and partly because they demonstrate Madonna’s ability to reinvent herself. At 67 years old, she can still create startling new material, even when, in the case of ‘Confessions II’, she is re-visiting her own past. Bravo! And viva Madonna!

posts.caption:
Arjun in Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara was a portrait of the workaholic millennial (are they okay??). It was meant to expose the absurdity of a high-profile corporate life that demanded constant availability. The kind of life tethered to every corner of the world and dictated by schedules across time zones. Watching it in 2011, you thought: who joins a client video call in the middle of a road trip? You nodded along with Laila, “Insaan ko dibbe mein sirf tab hona chahiye jab woh mar chuka ho.”
Do you laugh at your own naïveté now that your vacation has turned into eat, pray, Teams?
Once a physical location, the office is now something far more elusive. Stepping out of an office door meant shutting shop for the day. Work was confined by actual walls. But now that your phone is practically your conjoined twin, clocking out feels more like a corporate myth. Physical accessibility is no longer a limitation. Whether you’re on a beach in the Maldives or at a breakfast buffet in a Hyatt, any place can become a conference room if you put your mind to it. The only holiday epiphany you’re likely to have is the realisation that somewhere between your employment contract and your inbox, you signed your soul and calendar away.
DISCUSS ☕️✍🏽
posts.alt_text: Did they eat or no? ☕️👀😈

posts.caption:
Wait, I was about to type “generate visuals if Ex Machina was set in Old Delhi” into my ChatGuPTa, but VegNonVeg had already sent me these 🕹️💅😌
A collaboration between the OG Indian sneaker concept store and the brand that made audio-tech f*cking cool. VNV turns 10, marking a decade of giving Indian sneakerheads (you know who you are) a dedicated space at the dawn of hypebeast culture. With their new Beats collaboration, they’ve tapped into a category I’d call editorial headphones.
Because let’s be honest, it has never been just about sound quality. It matters how headphones look on a person. By integrating Indian silver jewellery, a craft tradition with its own sonic history, they’ve created the accessory for the tech generation.
Everyone wish VegNonVeg a happy burrrdayyy! 🎂
posts.alt_text: Did they eat or no? ☕️👀😈

posts.caption: Move your screen-printed saris and solid chart-paper backdrop photoshoots out of the way baby. The style criminal record has been left squeaky clean hennyy. Not a single allegation of a “chop” lewk. And now even the photoshoots? The cheetah on the left, the Ashoka pillar on the right. Rule of thirds apply. Compositions not seen since the Renaissance lmao. Pure cinema. I cannot vouch for the movie, but surely there will be no mixed reviews for these press lewks.
posts.alt_text: Did they eat or no? ☕️👀😈

posts.caption:
S is for Sarasa Textiles.
The label was founded by designer Maribel George after she found herself “unable to let go” of the work she was doing as part of the Venkatagiri Revival Project. They understand that one cannot claim to champion craft remotely from plush air-conditioned offices in tier-one cities, and has instead chosen to root itself in Venkatagiri itself. Here, they study the environment in which the cotton is woven, the women who wear it, and the myriad ways it is draped.
In a commercial landscape, where brands move from one emotional-support craft to another within a matter of months, Sarasa’s collections are developed over a span of up to four years, shaped by rigorous experimentation, historical inquiry, and deep textile study. Think Kanchipuram, Madras checks, and ikat enriched with exquisite zari, all woven in the quiet luxury (complimentary) of non-starched cotton, with saris ranging between ₹12,000 and ₹40,000.
Their campaigns, much like their saris, are strikingly contemporary yet steeped in nostalgia. They evoke the feeling of returning home. Like leafing through an old family photo album and finding elegant women in beautiful saris nestled between rain-soaked landscapes and images of lovingly prepared meals.
Sarasa might be making the most exquisite cotton saris, but what’s stands more brilliant is the complete abandonment of the fast modern fashion supply chain and still being able to be much more desirable than most bigger brands the market has to offer.

posts.caption:
Waiiittt! Is the printed sari back in conversation? Because right now stylists are not giving a second glance to a garment if it does not come with an endangered tradition tag. But Kangu walking out in this printed number for promotions, with 60s hairdo and rows of pearls to clutch and Miss Passi saying “hold my Leiber”, and pulling out a throwback in the same lewk (hair, pearls and all)? Much needed shade in this taapti dhoop lmaoooooo
But remember the 2010s?? When the coolest bunch of the millennial girlies brought the graphic printed sari to the mainstream at the dawn of Instagram. When it was all about statement prints and people went nuts with their Adobe softwares. If you could think it you probably printed it. And sari was not left behind. 9 yards of silk (or cheeky polyester lol) emblazoned with flowers, lipsticks, calligraphy, confectionaries and even Mickey Mouse (the Satya Paul and Disney collab anyone?). Anything that was under the sun could be on the sari. Probably the most expressive the garment ever was (?) DISCUSS ☕️👀💅🫦

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
On @kirandeep_chahal: LOVE ring in white gold & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in white gold, sapphires, & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in rose gold, pink sapphires & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in yellow gold, tsavorite garnets & diamonds, all by @cartier. Mala top by @raw_mango. Miss Z Riviera heels by @christianlouboutin.

She talks about stepping back, choosing carefully, and what she no longer offers up. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the full story.
.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega

posts.caption:
#ELLECulture: There are dysfunctional families, and then there’s ‘Maa Behen’, a film so gloriously chaotic that even its title sparks debate at the dinner table. Directed by Suresh Triveni (@sureshtriveni_) and led by a wildly uninhibited Madhuri Dixit (@madhuridixitnene), the film leans into emotional disorder, uncomfortable truths, and the kind of humour that makes you laugh before you realise you probably shouldn’t be laughing at all. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the full conversation.
Interview by: @vishakha_punjabi
[Maa Behen, Madhuri Dixit]

posts.caption:
#ELLECulture: “You seem pretty sad for a girl in love” has been out for barely a few days, yet it's already sent the internet into collective emotional chaos. Olivia Rodrigo has once again proven that no one weaponises vulnerability quite like she does, serving lyrics so painfully accurate they feel ripped straight from your Notes app at 2 a.m. From lyric breakdowns and Instagram edits to memes and fan theories, everyone is revisiting old relationships, questioning their dating history, and wondering if they were the problem all along.
[ You Seem Pretty Sad For A Girl So In Love, Olivia Rodrigo, Fan Reactions, OR 3]

posts.caption:
#ELLEFashion: Rooted in Leh and reaching far beyond it, Namza Couture is staying completely true to where they come from while making Ladakh’s textile story impossible to ignore. Founded by Padma Yangchan and Jigmet Disket, the label was never about aesthetics alone. It was about reframing a culture that had long been reduced to costume, and giving it the depth, dignity, and design language it always deserved.
Words by: @anamm__24
#Ladakhi #NazmaCouture #HeritageFashion

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw

posts.alt_text:
If you find yourself wondering if jeans really are fashion's latest luxury obsession, some of the greatest houses all say “yes.”

Just look at the material: @dior's embellished pairs in its fall/winter 2026 collection, @maisonalaia's denim launch, and @matthieu_blazy's brilliant use of jeans in @chanelofficial's commutercore Métiers d’art show, among others, have made the rough-and-tumble fabric runway-ready again. Even designers who weren’t necessarily associated with denim, like the always-ethereal @erdem, have entered the frayed fray. Now, more than ever, interest in—and the pricing of—jeans is skyrocketing. As denim expert @janepageherman tells ELLE: “In the last 20 years, people really figured out how to design jeans.”

Tap the link in bio for a full look at the return of high-end denim—and just how long the moment will last on and off the runway.

posts.alt_text:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.

Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”

For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
At 75, @maxmara proves that great style only gets better with age.
Today in Shanghai, creative director Ian Griffiths presented the cruise 2027 collection before an audience immersed in “The Max!,” the exhibition at the Long Museum celebrating the house’s remarkable journey, as Katie Holmes, @eileengu, and more stars looked on from the front row. As models wove through artifacts of the brand—archival sketches, heritage coats, and decades of imagery arranged on industrial shelving—a fresh expression of the house’s enduring codes came into focus.
Read our full review of the show at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Fresh off his powerhouse turn in Bison Kaalamaadan, cover star Dhruv Vikram (@dhruv.vikram) is entering a defining new phase. The son of superstar Vikram quite literally wears his legacy under his sleeve, with a tattoo of his father’s name on his wrist. Still showing flashes of the little boy who grew up believing he was a hero too, he speaks to us about ambition, expectation and the long road to becoming his own man.

posts.caption:
The first upset of FIFA World Cup 2026 (@fifaworldcup) is one fans will remember for years to come. On their World Cup debut, tiny Cabo Verde held European champions Spain to a stunning 0-0 draw, with 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha (@vozinha1) producing a heroic display to frustrate one of football's most feared attacks. In a tournament built on dreams and giant-killings, the African debutants have already delivered a result for the history books, and perhaps the first truly unforgettable shock of FIFA 2026.
#FIFAWorldCup2026 #CaboVerde #Spain #WorldCup2026

posts.caption: My outfit definitely expresses how I’m feeling. I might get extra done up if I’m feeling bad or in a funk and I don’t feel good when I’m not put together. Also I love style and fashion it’s like art for me putting outfits together. But there are many people that just get dressed and don’t think about it one way or another so I’m not sure that it affects everyone in the same way.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption:
#FeminaBusiness: Most brands spend years protecting their first idea. Lady Gaga (@ladygaga) did the opposite.
When Haus Labs (@hauslabs) launched in 2019, it entered the beauty industry with enormous expectations. But instead of clinging to what wasn’t working, Gaga chose reinvention, rebuilding the brand from the ground up and relaunching it as Haus Labs by Lady Gaga.
Today, the brand stands as one of the most compelling comeback stories in beauty: a reminder that success isn’t always about getting it right the first time. Sometimes, it’s about having the vision, and the courage to start again.
Because reinvention has always been Lady Gaga’s greatest art form.
#Femina #LadyGaga #HausLabs #BeautyIndustry

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
Looking to support more sustainable Black-owned brands? We have a list for you 💫⁠
⁠
Whether you’re hoping to shop small for #Juneteenth or are looking for more diverse brands in sustainability, this list of eleven brands is for you—and is a reminder that more sustainable fashion means a fashion world that is diverse, is equitable, and is inclusive 💚⁠
⁠
In conversations about sustainability, the subject almost always defaults to the environment and materials but, like garment workers, true sustainability doesn’t happen without pushing toward change that considers everyone. Understanding this and that we need to uplift creatives and workers of all backgrounds, we can achieve true sustainability.⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about brands like @studiooneeightynine, @samimiro, and @lemlemofficial before exploring the full list at the link. Which Black-owned brands would you like to see rated? Share in the comments ✏️⁠
⁠
#BlackOwnedBrand #ShopSmall #SustainableFashion #Sustainability
